source: This Week in Startups — E1990: Liquidity Summit fireside with Antonio Gracias and Gavin Baker
summary: Foundation model companies without unique proprietary data AND internet-scale distribution are the fastest depreciating assets in human history and will go to zero.
summary_extended: Baker argues that foundation model companies lacking both unique proprietary data AND internet-scale distribution are the fastest depreciating assets in human history and will ultimately go to zero. His reasoning is that without differentiated data or a massive distribution moat, these companies cannot compete as models commoditize, leaving them with no durable competitive advantage and no path to monetization. He estimates there are roughly ten such companies — naming examples like Inflection, Mistral, Cohere, AI21 Labs, and Stability AI — and believes most will be zeros. The only realistic exit for most is an acqui-hire, citing Microsoft's absorption of Inflection as the template, and suggesting Amazon is the most likely acquirer for the remaining candidates. The investment implication is to avoid these private foundation model companies entirely and instead favor the hyperscalers with distribution (MSFT, AMZN) who can absorb talent and IP at distressed prices.
detail: Baker states this as a core conviction: if you are a foundation model company and lack both unique data and internet-scale distribution, you are worthless. He estimates there are ~10 such companies and most will be zeros. The only exit path for most is an acqui-hire by Amazon (as Microsoft did with Inflection).
confidence_evidence: Baker uses the phrase 'fastest depreciating asset in human history' and 'most of these companies are zeros' — extremely strong language; this is a core framework he applies.
quote_fragment: fastest depreciating asset in human history... most of these companies are zeros

ticker_audit.reasoning: Microsoft is explicitly cited as the template acquirer (Inflection acqui-hire) and as a hyperscaler with both proprietary data and internet-scale distribution — exactly the moat Baker says foundation model pure-plays lack. MSFT directly benefits as distressed foundation model talent and IP flows to it at low cost.

Baker explicitly names Amazon as the most likely acquirer for the remaining foundation model companies that need an exit, making it a direct beneficiary of the thesis — it gains talent and technology cheaply as these companies fail.
